lessons.title: Array Basics
lessons.summary: Learn how to declare, initialize, and access arrays

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to declare, initialize, and access elements of an [[array]]
- The rule that indexes (subscripts) start from 0
- How to get the length of an array (length)

Arrays are the most fundamental mechanism for "managing data of the same type together."

contents.title: Declaring and Initializing Arrays
contents.body: There are three main ways to create an array.
contents.note: The size of an array cannot be changed after creation. If you need a resizable collection, use [[ArrayList]] (covered in a later lesson).

contents.title: Accessing Elements and length
contents.body: Array elements are accessed using an index (a number starting from 0).
contents.note: length is a field, not a [[method]], so no parentheses are needed. This differs from [[String]]'s length(), which is a method.

contents.title: Arrays and for Loops
contents.body: Use a [[for loop]] to process all elements of an array.
contents.note: The [[enhanced for loop]] provides a concise way to iterate when you don't need the index. However, use a standard for loop when you need to modify elements or reference the index.

contents.title: Array Indexes Start at 0
contents.body:
This is the most important rule about arrays.

| Element | 1st | 2nd | 3rd | nth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Index | 0 | 1 | 2 | n-1 |

- An array of size 5 → indexes range from 0 to 4
- Accessing a non-existent index throws **ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException**

This is a frequently encountered error in both exams and real-world development.
contents.note: The last element of an array is accessed with array[array.length - 1].

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Arrays store data of the same type in a fixed-size container
- Indexes start at 0; the last element is array[length - 1]
- length is a field (no parentheses needed)
- Out-of-bounds access throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
- The enhanced for loop (for-each) provides concise iteration over all elements
- Array size cannot be changed after creation

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: An array created with new int[3] is initialized with the default value 0. Since 5 is assigned to a[1], a[0]=0, a[1]=5, a[2]=0.

quizzes.question: Which line in the following code causes an error?
quizzes.explanation: The length of an array is a field, not a method. Adding parentheses as length() causes a compilation error. The correct syntax is colors.length. Note that String's length() is a method and requires parentheses, but arrays are different.
The index of the last element of an array is length - 1. length is a field that returns the number of elements in the array. In this array, length is 3, so the last element is nums[2] (= 30).

lessons.title: Multidimensional Arrays and the Arrays Class
lessons.summary: Two-dimensional arrays and array utility methods

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- How to work with 2D [[array]]s (table-like data)
- Useful [[method]]s such as Arrays.sort() and Arrays.toString()
- Important notes on copying and comparing arrays

With the Arrays class, you can accomplish in a single line what used to require manual coding.

contents.title: Two-Dimensional Arrays
contents.body: A 2D array manages data using "rows and columns." It is ideal for tabular data.
contents.note: Java's 2D arrays can also be jagged arrays where each row has a different number of columns. For example: int[][] jagged = {{1,2}, {3,4,5}, {6}};

contents.title: Key Methods of the Arrays Class
contents.body: The java.util.Arrays class provides useful methods for array operations.
contents.note: If you print an array directly with println, it displays a hash code like [I@1a2b3c4. Use Arrays.toString() to see the contents.

contents.title: Binary Search and Partial Sort
contents.body: You can also perform fast searches on sorted arrays and partial sorts.
contents.note: Always use binarySearch on a sorted array. Using it on an unsorted array will not produce correct results.

contents.title: You Can't Copy an Array with "="
contents.body:
Assigning an array with = does not copy its contents—it creates a "reference to the same array."

| Method | Behavior |
|---|---|
| b = a | Shares the same array (changing b also changes a) |
| b = Arrays.copyOf(a, a.length) | Creates an independent copy |
| b = a.clone() | Creates an independent copy |

This relates to the important concept of "[[reference types]]." You will learn more about this in Chapter 7.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Two-dimensional array: access with matrix[row][column]
- matrix.length for row count, matrix[i].length for column count
- Arrays.sort() for ascending sort
- Arrays.toString() to convert array contents to a string
- Arrays.copyOf() to copy (with optional size change)
- Arrays.fill() to fill all elements with the same value
- Arrays.equals() for content comparison (== compares references, which is incorrect)
- Arrays.binarySearch() for fast search on sorted arrays

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: m.length is the number of rows, which is 3, and m[0].length is the number of columns in row 0, which is 2. In a 2D array, length returns the "number of rows," and each row's length returns the "number of columns."

quizzes.question: What is the correct way to compare the contents of two arrays?
quizzes.explanation: Use Arrays.equals() to compare array contents. The == operator compares references (memory addresses), so it returns false even if the contents are the same but the objects are different. a.equals(b) calls Object's equals, which produces the same result as ==.

Arrays.sort() sorts an array in ascending order. Dual-pivot quicksort is used for primitive arrays, and TimSort is used for object arrays. The original array is modified in place (no new array is created).

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- **ArrayList**: a list that automatically resizes
- Basic operations such as add, get, remove, and size
- When to use [[array]]s vs. [[ArrayList]]

ArrayList is one of the most frequently used data structures in Java development.

contents.title: The List Interface and ArrayList
contents.body:
List is an [[interface]] in Java's Collections Framework.

| Class | Characteristics |
|---|---|
| List | Ordered, allows duplicates, index-accessible |
| ArrayList | List implementation backed by an internal array (fastest and most common) |
| LinkedList | List implementation using linked nodes (fast insertion/deletion) |

Best practice is to declare [[variable]]s using the List interface type.
  ○ List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
  △ ArrayList<String> list = new ArrayList<>();

Reasons for declaring with the interface type:
- Makes it easy to change the implementation [[class]] later
- More versatile for [[method]] parameters and return types
contents.note: The [[generics]] <String> means "this List can only contain String elements." The <> ([[diamond operator]]) enables [[type inference]] on the right side.

contents.title: Differences Between ArrayList and Arrays
contents.body:
Use these criteria when deciding which one to use:

| Feature | Array | ArrayList |
|---|---|---|
| Size | Fixed (set at creation) | Variable (grows automatically) |
| Primitive types | Can store | Cannot store (wrapper types required) |
| Utility methods | Few | Rich (add, remove, contains, etc.) |
| Performance | Slightly faster | Slightly slower |

In practice, ArrayList (List) is used in most cases.
contents.note: **ArrayList**<int> causes an error. Use ArrayList<Integer> instead (wrapper class).

contents.title: Basic ArrayList Operations
contents.body: Let's learn the key methods of ArrayList.
contents.note: For List<Integer>, remove(1) means "remove the element at index 1." To remove by value, use remove(Integer.valueOf(1)).

contents.title: Differences Between ArrayList and LinkedList
contents.body: Choose based on your use case.
contents.note: In practice, ArrayList is used in most cases. [[LinkedList]] is mainly used as a queue or [[stack]].

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Declaring variables with the List<E> interface is recommended
| Item | Description |
|---|---|
| ArrayList | Array-based, O(1) random access, most common |
| LinkedList | Link-based, O(1) insertion at head |
| Key methods | add, get, set, remove, size, contains, indexOf, isEmpty, clear |
- size() is a method (unlike array's length, which is a field)
- The <> diamond [[operator]] enables type inference on the right side

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: add(1, "X") inserts "X" at index 1. Elements at index 1 and beyond are shifted to the right. The result is [A, X, B, C]. add(value) appends to the end, while add(index, value) inserts at the specified position.

quizzes.question: Which is the most recommended way to declare an ArrayList variable?
quizzes.explanation: Declaring variables using the List interface type is a Java best practice. This way, even if you change the implementation class from ArrayList to LinkedList, you only need to modify the variable declaration (the principle of "programming to an interface").
The number of elements in a List is obtained using the size() method. Note that arrays use the length field, String uses the length() method, and List uses the size() method—each is different.

quizzes.question: Complete the code to add an element to an ArrayList and get its size.
quizzes.explanation: ArrayList is an implementation of the List interface. Use add() to add elements and size() to get the element count. This differs from array's length.

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The key feature of Set: no duplicates allowed
- How to use [[HashSet]] and its important considerations
- How to quickly check "has this already been added?"

When you need to manage unique values, Set is the ideal choice.

contents.title: Basic HashSet Operations
contents.body: HashSet is the most common implementation of the Set interface.
contents.note: HashSet does not guarantee order. Use LinkedHashSet if you need to preserve insertion order.

contents.title: TreeSet and LinkedHashSet
contents.body: Each Set implementation [[class]] has its own characteristics.
contents.note: [[TreeSet]] uses a red-black tree internally, and its elements must implement [[Comparable]]. Standard types like [[String]] and Integer already implement Comparable.

contents.title: HashSet Does Not Guarantee Order
contents.body:
Elements in a HashSet may not be retrieved in the order they were added.

| Collection | Duplicates | Order |
|---|---|---|
| ArrayList | Allowed | Preserves insertion order |
| HashSet | Not allowed | Unordered |
| LinkedHashSet | Not allowed | Preserves insertion order |
| TreeSet | Not allowed | Natural order (sorted) |

If you need ordering, use LinkedHashSet or TreeSet.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Set is a collection that does not allow duplicates
| Class | Characteristics |
|---|---|
| HashSet | Fastest, unordered (most common) |
| TreeSet | Sorted in natural order |
| LinkedHashSet | Preserves insertion order |
- contains() provides O(1) fast existence checks (HashSet)
- Remove duplicates from a List: new HashSet<>(list)
- retainAll() for intersection, removeAll() for difference

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: HashSet does not allow duplicates, so adding "Java" twice results in only one entry. The result is 3 elements: "Java", "Python", and "Go".

quizzes.question: Which Set implementation keeps elements in sorted order?
quizzes.explanation: TreeSet uses a Red-Black Tree internally to keep elements sorted in natural order (ascending). HashSet has no defined order, and LinkedHashSet preserves insertion order. SortedSet is an interface, and TreeSet is its implementation.

quizzes.question: Which is the correct way to remove duplicates from a List?
quizzes.explanation: Passing a List to the HashSet constructor automatically removes duplicates. new HashSet<>(list) creates a Set without duplicates. This technique leverages the inherent property of Set data structures that do not allow duplicate elements.

quizzes.question: Arrange the basic ArrayList operations in the correct order.
quizzes.explanation: ArrayList operations follow the order: create → add → remove → check size. After remove(0), size() returns 1.

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Map: managing data as "key-value pairs"
- Basic operations: put, get, containsKey, keySet
- How to represent data like a dictionary or phone book

Map is a "data structure searchable by name" that is extremely common in practice.

contents.title: Basic HashMap Operations
contents.body: [[HashMap]] is the most common implementation of the Map interface.
contents.note: Map keys must be unique (no duplicates). Calling put() with the same key overwrites the value.

contents.title: Useful Map Methods and TreeMap
contents.body: Here are useful [[method]]s added since Java 8 and how to use [[TreeMap]].
contents.note: TreeMap sorts by the natural order of keys. HashMap has no defined order, and LinkedHashMap preserves insertion order. Remember the correspondence between Set and Map implementation [[class]]es.

contents.title: Choosing the Right Collection Implementation
contents.body:
Choose the best collection for your purpose.

**List types**: Ordered, allows duplicates
- **ArrayList**: Most common. Random access ○, insertion/deletion △
- [[LinkedList]]: Insertion/deletion at head ○, random access △

**Set types**: Unordered, no duplicates
| Class | Characteristics |
|---|---|
| HashSet | Fastest. No defined order |
| TreeSet | Sorted order |
| LinkedHashSet | Insertion order |

**Map types**: Key-value pairs
| Class | Characteristics |
|---|---|
| HashMap | Fastest. No defined key order |
| TreeMap | Sorted by key |
| LinkedHashMap | Insertion order |

When in doubt: choose [[ArrayList]] / [[HashSet]] / HashMap!

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Map manages key-value pairs. Keys must be unique
- put(key, value) to add, get(key) to retrieve
- getOrDefault() to avoid null
- entrySet() to loop over key-value pairs
- putIfAbsent() does not overwrite existing keys
- HashMap → fastest, TreeMap → sorted by key, LinkedHashMap → insertion order

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Calling put() with the same key "X" overwrites the value. After the first put("X", 10), put("X", 30) updates the value to 30. There are 2 keys ("X" and "Y"), so size() returns 2.

quizzes.question: What is the return value when calling get() with a key that does not exist?
quizzes.explanation: When a non-existent key is specified in Map's get(), null is returned. To avoid NullPointerException, you can either check with containsKey() beforehand or use getOrDefault(key, defaultValue).

quizzes.question:
Fill in the blank with the method used to loop over key-value pairs of a Map.
for (Map.Entry<String, Integer> e : map._____()) {
quizzes.explanation: entrySet() returns all entries (key-value pairs) of a Map as a Set. From each Map.Entry, getKey() retrieves the key and getValue() retrieves the value. keySet() returns only keys, and values() returns only values.

quizzes.question: Select all correct statements about ArrayList.
quizzes.explanation: ArrayList is a variable-length array that allows adding and removing elements. You can specify types using generics. Random access by index is O(1) and fast. However, insertion and deletion require shifting elements, making them O(n). Primitive types cannot be stored directly—wrapper classes must be used.

choices.text: Elements can be added and removed
Index access is O(1) and fast
Primitive types can be stored directly
Types can be specified using generics

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Creating immutable collections with **List.of**() and Map.of()
- The difference from [[Collections]].unmodifiableList()
- The benefits of using immutable collections (improved safety)

You will learn design patterns that protect data from unintended modifications.

contents.title: Immutable Collections (List.of / Set.of / Map.of)
contents.body: Since Java 9, factory [[method]]s can create unmodifiable collections.
contents.note: **List.of**() does not allow null elements. If you need a list that contains null, use [[ArrayList]].

contents.title: Modification Operations on Immutable Collections Throw UnsupportedOperationException
contents.body:
Collections created with **List.of**() or Map.of() cannot be modified.

- Calling add, remove, put, etc. throws UnsupportedOperationException
- Including a null element throws [[NullPointerException]]

By "making data that should not change immutable," you can prevent bugs caused by unintended modifications.
contents.note: To make an immutable collection mutable: new **ArrayList**<>(**List.of**(1, 2, 3))

contents.title: The Collections Utility Class
contents.body: Here are useful methods from the java.util.Collections class.
contents.note: Collections.unmodifiableList() wraps an existing List to make it unmodifiable. Unlike Java 9+'s **List.of**(), modifications to the original List will be reflected in the wrapped list.

contents.title: Converting Between Arrays and Lists
contents.body: Here's how to convert between [[array]]s and Lists.
contents.note: The List created by Arrays.asList() has a fixed size. You cannot add or remove elements, but modification via set() is allowed. It is also linked to the original array, so changes to one are reflected in the other.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- **List.of**() / Set.of() / Map.of(): immutable collections (Java 9+)
- Immutable collections cannot contain null
- Collections.sort() to sort a list
- Collections.reverseOrder() for reverse order
- Collections.unmodifiableList() for an unmodifiable wrapper
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| Arrays.asList() | Array → fixed-size List (no add/remove) |
| new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(arr)) | Convert to a mutable List |
| list.toArray(new Type[0]) | List → array |

quizzes.question: Which operation is possible on a list created with List.of("A", "B", "C")?
quizzes.explanation: An immutable list created with List.of() cannot be modified. add(), remove(), set(), and clear() all throw UnsupportedOperationException. However, get() for reading is allowed.

quizzes.question: Which statement correctly describes a List created with Arrays.asList()?
quizzes.explanation: Arrays.asList() returns a fixed-size List. add() and remove() throw UnsupportedOperationException, but modifying existing elements with set() is allowed. It is also linked to the original array, so changes affect both directions.

choices.text: Adding, removing, and modifying elements are all possible
Adding and removing are not possible, but modifying is
Behaves exactly the same as List.of()

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Collections.frequency() returns how many times a specified element appears in the given collection. The list contains "A" three times (at indexes 0, 2, and 4), so 3 is output.

contents.title: What You'll Learn
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The role of wrapper [[class]]es (Integer, Double, etc.)
- How [[autoboxing]] and unboxing work
- When to use primitive types vs. wrapper classes

This knowledge is essential for storing numbers in collections.

contents.title: Wrapper Class List
contents.body:
Each [[primitive type]] has a corresponding wrapper class.

| Primitive Type | Wrapper Class |
|---|---|
| byte | Byte |
| short | Short |
| int | Integer |
| long | Long |
| float | Float |
| double | Double |
| char | Character |
| boolean | Boolean |

Key points to remember:
- The wrapper for int is Integer (not Int)
- The wrapper for char is Character (not Char)
- All others simply capitalize the first letter (double → Double)
contents.note: All wrapper classes belong to the java.lang package and can be used without importing.

contents.title: Autoboxing Performance Considerations
contents.body:
Autoboxing is convenient, but be careful when handling large amounts of data.

- Converting int → Integer may create a new object each time
- Frequent conversions inside loops can degrade performance
- Use equals() instead of == to compare Integer objects

In particular, == comparison for Integer only works reliably within the range -128 to 127.
contents.note: A common exam question: "Integer a = 127; Integer b = 127; a == b → true" but "Integer a = 128; Integer b = 128; a == b → false".

contents.title: Autoboxing and Unboxing
contents.body: Since Java 5, conversions between primitive types and wrapper classes happen automatically.
contents.note: Unboxing a null value causes a [[NullPointerException]]. Be careful in situations where Map's get() may return null.

contents.title: Useful Wrapper Class Methods
contents.body: Wrapper classes provide useful features such as [[type conversion]] and constants.
contents.note: Integer values in the range -128 to 127 are cached, so == returns true. Outside this range, == returns false. Always use equals() to compare wrapper classes.

contents.title: Key Points
contents.body:
- Collections cannot store primitive types directly
| Item | Description |
|---|---|
| Wrapper classes | int → Integer, char → Character (note the names) |
| Autoboxing | Automatic conversion from primitive → wrapper |
| Unboxing | Automatic conversion from wrapper → primitive |
- Unboxing null throws NullPointerException
- Integer.parseInt() converts a string to a number
- == comparison for Integer is only true for -128 to 127 → use equals()
- Integer.MAX_VALUE / MIN_VALUE to get maximum and minimum values

quizzes.question: What is the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Integer caches values in the range -128 to 127, so 100 reuses the same object and a == b is true. However, 200 is outside the cache range, so separate objects are created and c == d is false. Always use equals() to compare wrapper classes.

quizzes.question: What is the wrapper class for the int type?
quizzes.explanation: The wrapper class for int is Integer. It is not Int (which doesn't exist) or Number (which is an abstract class). Only int and char have names that change significantly—to Integer and Character respectively. All other primitive types simply capitalize the first letter.

quizzes.question: Which line in the following code causes a runtime error?
quizzes.explanation: Line 3 attempts to unbox a null Integer to int, causing a NullPointerException. println can output null as the string "null", so it does not throw an error. During unboxing, intValue() is called internally, but a method cannot be called on a null object.
